episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Repair Culture & Practical Tech
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Repair Culture & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [New Atlas] Cheap metacrystal panels could easily steer wireless signals around barriers and [BC Gov News] Canada and British Columbia forge new partnership to accelerate homebuilding, lower costs and build new local infrastructure, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What institutional and structural mechanisms can sustain repair knowledge and culture in rural communities when informal networks are fragile and formal funding is unreliable? Topics include: Right-to-repair legislation and manufacturer compliance gaps (Apple Self Service Repair model), Rural repair knowledge succession crisis and aging skilled workers, Community repair infrastructure models (repair cafés, tool libraries, cooperatives, time banking), Indigenous knowledge systems and land stewardship as durable repair/technical knowledge transmission, AI and software dependency as modern repair-culture analog (local inference, vendor lock-in).

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Association for Community Living

discussion.central_question: What institutional and structural mechanisms can sustain repair knowledge and culture in rural communities when informal networks are fragile and formal funding is unreliable?
discussion.riley_position: Regulation requiring parts access and documentation is essential and separate from the skill-gap problem; community models like tool libraries, repair cafés, and cooperatives can create low-cost infrastructure for knowledge transfer if designed properly.
riley_key_evidence: EU's right-to-repair directive came into force in 2024 covering phones, laptops, and home appliances
Fairphone 5 scores 10/10 on iFixit repairability index; Framework laptops are user-serviceable from design
Toronto Public Library and smaller BC systems have integrated tool lending programs
discussion.casey_position: Informal repair networks and community programs are fragile without permanent institutional backing; rural communities face succession problems where aging repair knowledge holders retire without formal mechanisms for transfer, and cooperative/time-banking models struggle with operational costs and valuation disagreements.
casey_key_evidence: The Cariboo has lost institutional repair knowledge over two decades due to lack of formal succession planning
Tool libraries consistently fail to scale due to liability, replacement costs, and staff management expenses
Time banking fails when communities disagree on valuation—is an hour of electrical work equal to an hour of childcare?
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey agreed on the underlying structural problem but acknowledged different solution levels: regulation and documentation are necessary but insufficient; Indigenous knowledge systems represent durable models that maintain transmission outside Western institutional funding; the real question is identifying who holds critical knowledge in each community and ensuring succession before those people leave.
topics_covered: Right-to-repair legislation and manufacturer compliance gaps (Apple Self Service Repair model)
Rural repair knowledge succession crisis and aging skilled workers
Community repair infrastructure models (repair cafés, tool libraries, cooperatives, time banking)
Indigenous knowledge systems and land stewardship as durable repair/technical knowledge transmission
AI and software dependency as modern repair-culture analog (local inference, vendor lock-in)
